original_hypothesis.title: lockPermanent Missing Reentrancy Guard Enables Callback Exploitation via Voter.poke
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: lockPermanent and unlockPermanent lack the nonreentrant modifier. lockPermanent calls IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) when voted[_tokenId] is true, which makes external calls to Bribe contracts (deposit/withdraw). If any of these external calls have a callback mechanism (e.g., via token hooks or contract receiver), state could be manipulated between the permanentLockBalance update and the checkpoint/storage write.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: All other state-modifying functions in VotingEscrow use nonreentrant. lockPermanent modifies permanentLockBalance and locked[_tokenId] and then calls external contracts via voter.poke. The absence of reentrancy protection is inconsistent with the rest of the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lockPermanent modifies permanentLockBalance and locked[_tokenId], calls _checkpoint, stores locked[_tokenId], then calls external IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) - all without nonreentrant modifier

preconditions: A callback mechanism exists in the poke -> bribe deposit/withdraw chain
Bribe token or receiving contract has a callback (e.g., ERC777 hook)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: permanentLockBalance += _amount is written before poke external call. If reentered, permanentLockBalance could be double-incremented.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Reenter lockPermanent during poke's external calls to double-count permanentLockBalance, inflating totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Inflated permanentLockBalance increases totalSupply, affecting rebase distribution ratios

economic_questions: Is there any callback path in the poke -> bribe deposit chain that returns control to VotingEscrow?
Could a malicious bribe token trigger reentrancy?
What is the concrete impact of double-incrementing permanentLockBalance?
validation_requirements: Trace the full external call chain from lockPermanent -> poke -> _vote -> _reset -> bribe.withdraw/deposit
Check if any bribe token could have hooks (ERC777, callback on transfer)
Test if VoterV3's nonReentrant blocks reentry into poke path

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal - only needs a veNFT with non-permanent lock
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on whether reentrancy path exists

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time when token has voted
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Would corrupt global voting power accounting

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All veNFT holders via distorted totalSupply

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Trace all external calls from lockPermanent
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify if VoterV3 nonReentrant blocks reentrancy back to VE

validation_result.mechanism: missing reentrancy guard on state-modifying function with external calls
validation_result.impact_summary: lockPermanent modifies critical global state (permanentLockBalance, locked[], checkpoint data) then makes external calls via IVoter(voter).poke() without the nonreentrant modifier, inconsistent with all other state-modifying functions in VotingEscrow.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that lockPermanent and unlockPermanent lack the nonreentrant modifier present on all other state-modifying functions (deposit_for, create_lock, increase_amount, increase_unlock_time, withdraw, merge, split). lockPermanent modifies permanentLockBalance, locked[_tokenId], and checkpoint data, then makes external calls via IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId). However, the specific exploitation path described (callbacks from Bribe.deposit/withdraw) is not feasible because those Bribe functions make NO external calls - they only update internal accounting state. The state updates in lockPermanent follow checks-effects-interactions (all writes before the poke call), and re-entry with the same tokenId is blocked by require(!_newLocked.isPermanent). Nevertheless, the missing modifier IS a code defect: it violates the contract's own defensive pattern and leaves the function unprotected against future changes to the Voter/GaugeManager/Bribe code that might introduce callback vectors. The GaugeManager source code is out of scope, and its functions (fetchInternalBribeFromPool, fetchExternalBribeFromPool, isGaugeAliveForPool) called during poke represent potential callback vectors whose safety cannot be verified.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: lockPermanent at VotingEscrow.sol lines ~492-511 has no nonreentrant modifier. Compare with deposit_for (line ~431: 'external nonreentrant'), create_lock (line ~463: 'external nonreentrant'), increase_amount (line ~469: 'external nonreentrant'), increase_unlock_time (line ~483: 'external nonreentrant'), withdraw (line ~520: 'external nonreentrant'), merge (line ~560: 'external nonreentrant'), split (line ~617: 'external nonreentrant'). The function updates permanentLockBalance before calling poke, which triggers external calls to GaugeManager and Bribe contracts. Bribe.deposit and Bribe.withdraw (Bribes.sol) do NOT make external calls, so the specific callback vector described is not feasible. However, VoterV3._vote calls gaugeManager.isGaugeAliveForPool, gaugeManager.fetchInternalBribeFromPool, and gaugeManager.fetchExternalBribeFromPool - all external calls to out-of-scope code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing nonreentrant modifier. All other state-modifying functions in VotingEscrow use nonreentrant. This function modifies permanentLockBalance and locked[_tokenId], then makes external calls via IVoter(voter).poke(_tokenId) which calls into GaugeManager (out-of-scope code) and Bribe contracts.

Missing nonreentrant modifier for consistency, though unlockPermanent requires !voted[_tokenId] so it never calls poke and makes no external calls. Lower risk than lockPermanent but still inconsistent.

calling_sequence: 4. _checkpoint writes to global and user point_history (STATE CHANGE)
8. _vote calls _reset which calls IBribe.withdraw (no external calls in Bribe.withdraw)
9. _vote calls gaugeManager.isGaugeAliveForPool (external call to out-of-scope code)
11. _vote calls IBribe.deposit (no external calls in Bribe.deposit)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The lockPermanent function modifies critical state (permanentLockBalance, locked[], checkpoint data) at steps 2-5, then makes external calls at step 6 via poke. The VE contract's custom nonreentrant guard (_entered_state) is NOT applied to lockPermanent, so re-entry into lockPermanent (with a different tokenId) is not blocked. While VoterV3.poke has its own nonReentrant, and Bribe.deposit/withdraw make no external calls, the GaugeManager calls at steps 9-10 are to out-of-scope code whose callback behavior cannot be verified.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for VotingEscrow deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The specific attack described (callback via Bribe ERC777 hooks during deposit/withdraw) is NOT feasible because Bribe.deposit and Bribe.withdraw make zero external calls - they only update internal balanceOf, totalSupply, and checkpoints. No token transfers occur in these functions. However, the code defect (missing nonreentrant modifier) is real. The GaugeManager calls in the poke execution path are to an out-of-scope contract, and those represent unverifiable callback vectors. Re-entry with the same tokenId is blocked by the require(!_newLocked.isPermanent) check since locked[_tokenId] is updated before poke. Re-entry with a different tokenId would correctly increment permanentLockBalance for that different token.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The missing nonreentrant modifier is a genuine code defect that breaks the contract's defensive pattern. All 8 other state-modifying functions use nonreentrant, but lockPermanent and unlockPermanent do not. While current exploitation paths through Bribe callbacks are infeasible, the GaugeManager calls represent unverifiable external call surfaces. If GaugeManager or its downstream contracts are ever modified to include callbacks (or if a custom GaugeManager implementation provides them), the missing guard could become exploitable. The impact of potential exploitation would be corruption of permanentLockBalance and totalSupply calculations affecting rebase distribution and governance.
recommended_mitigations: Add the nonreentrant modifier to lockPermanent and unlockPermanent for consistency with all other state-modifying functions
Consider moving the poke external call to a separate function that users must call explicitly, removing the external call from lockPermanent entirely
If keeping the poke call, ensure the CEI pattern is strictly followed (already done for single-tokenId re-entry, but defense-in-depth via nonreentrant is recommended)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VotingEscrow, VoterV3, and Bribe contracts. 2. Create a malicious GaugeManager that provides a callback when fetchInternalBribeFromPool or isGaugeAliveForPool is called. 3. Create a lock, vote for a pool, then call lockPermanent. 4. In the callback from the malicious GaugeManager, attempt to call lockPermanent with a different tokenId. 5. Verify whether the VotingEscrow nonreentrant guard blocks re-entry (it won't, since the modifier is missing). 6. Alternatively, verify that VoterV3.nonReentrant blocks re-entry through poke from the callback.
uncertainties: GaugeManager implementation is out of scope - cannot verify if its functions (isGaugeAliveForPool, fetchInternalBribeFromPool, fetchExternalBribeFromPool) could provide callback vectors
Future modifications to Bribe or GaugeManager contracts could introduce callback mechanisms that exploit this missing guard
The VoterV3.nonReentrant protects re-entry into poke but does NOT protect re-entry into lockPermanent directly
additional_testing_needed: Verify GaugeManager source code for any callback mechanisms in isGaugeAliveForPool, fetchInternalBribeFromPool, fetchExternalBribeFromPool
Test re-entry into lockPermanent via a custom GaugeManager that calls back during poke execution
Verify that VotingEscrow's custom reentrancy guard (_entered_state) could be applied to lockPermanent without breaking the poke call flow
